title: Condition → training considerations
description: Baked condition→training CONSIDERATION notes (issue #666) — the calm, coaching-tier note shown on the next-workout suggestion when a mapped medical condition is active. CURATED HIGH-VALUE SUBSET, not exhaustive; facts from public ACOG/NIH/NHLBI/CDC and U.S. HHS physical-activity guidance. INFORMATIONAL, never prescriptive — a consideration, NEVER a prohibition; the recommendation is never gated or re-ranked by a condition (medical judgment stays with the clinician). Committed + HUMAN-REVIEWABLE; regenerate with `npm run gen:condition-training-considerations`.

citation.source: ACOG Committee Opinion 804 (pregnancy); NIH NIAMS (osteoporosis); NHLBI / CDC (hypertension, aortic aneurysm); U.S. HHS Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, 2nd ed.; NIH MedlinePlus.
citation.note: All public U.S. references; each entry additionally carries its own per-entry `source`. Curated high-value subset, not exhaustive. Consideration notes only — never prescriptive.

entries.note: You have pregnancy on file — continued exercise is beneficial, but favor stable positions and avoid supine work after the first trimester, contact/fall risk, and breath-holding under load. Discuss your training plan with your clinician.
entries.source: ACOG Committee Opinion 804: Physical Activity and Exercise During Pregnancy and the Postpartum Period; U.S. HHS Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, 2nd ed.

entries.note: You have osteoporosis on file — progressive resistance and weight-bearing loading is beneficial for bone. Favor controlled progressive loading and discuss high-impact work and heavy loaded spinal flexion with your clinician.
entries.source: NIH NIAMS: Exercise for Your Bone Health; U.S. HHS Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, 2nd ed.

entries.note: You have uncontrolled hypertension on file — regular aerobic and moderate resistance training helps lower blood pressure, but avoid maximal breath-holding (Valsalva) efforts, which spike blood pressure. Keep intensity moderate until it is controlled and discuss with your clinician.
entries.source: NHLBI / CDC blood-pressure management guidance; U.S. HHS Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, 2nd ed.

entries.note: You have a recent surgery on file — clearance to resume loaded training comes from your surgical team. Confirm you have been cleared, and ease back gradually rather than returning to prior loads at once.
entries.source: NIH MedlinePlus: Surgery recovery / activity after surgery.

entries.note: You have an aortic aneurysm on file — moderate aerobic activity is generally encouraged, but heavy maximal lifting and breath-holding (Valsalva) sharply raise blood pressure. Keep resistance work moderate and rep-based, and discuss safe limits with your clinician.
entries.source: NHLBI: Aortic aneurysm — living with; U.S. HHS Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, 2nd ed.
